issue_title: Missing shh.subscribe function on Whisper protocol
issue_body:
Whisper Protocol in original has a subscribe function, please see the [Whisper-v5-RPC-API](https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/wiki/Whisper-v5-RPC-API#shh_subscribe).

> **shh_subscribe**
> 
> Creates and registers a new subscription to receive notifications for inbound whisper messages. Returns the ID of the newly created subscription.

But Web3.py seems like does not have `shh.subscribe` function on its [documentation and implementation](http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web3.shh.html#module-web3.shh).

**=>** Would it be implemented? or is it not implemented in purpose?

issue_title: tdl.parse() should allow a filename or open file as its argument
issue_body:
`delphin.tdl.parse()` could be more user-friendly if can take a filename as its argument in addition to open file objects.

Note that currently `tdl.parse()` can work with any argument that iterates lines:
* open file objects
* `io.StringIO`-wrapped strings
* list of strings

I don't think the last one is really necessary, but if we do a check like the following, it will be excluded:

```python
def parse(f):
    if hasattr(f, 'read'):
        return _parse(f)
    else:
        with open(f) as fh:
            return _parse(fh)
```

Alternatively the check could be for string types, e.g.,

```python
from delphin.util import stringtypes
...
    if isinstance(f, stringtypes):
        with open(f) as fh:
            ...
```

Update:
* Don't worry about `tdl.lex()` for now.

issue_title: Make conda have an option modify the path to the dynamic linker/loader
issue_body:
Much like
`conda --set-interpreter /path/to/ld-linux.so`

I've encountered a problem while using Red Hat 7.1 with Linuxbrew and Conda. Red Hat 7.1 is an old distribution and Linuxbrew provide a solution for having more up-to-date libraries (in special, glibc, dynamic linker/loader, gcc compiler, stdc++)  for those without root privileges (common case in clusters).

However, Anaconda distribution had all its binaries pointing to the old /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, which means that python packages compiled later via the gcc compiler provided by linuxbrew wouldn't work as conda python would load the old glibc but those compiled libraries would request incompatible but newer infrastructure libs (like libstdc++) provided by linuxbrew.

Here is the link to the problem I found and a solution: https://github.com/Linuxbrew/brew/issues/77#issuecomment-232248602

Just a suggestion. Not sure if it makes sense or just complicate things as the problematic python package came from pip, and not from conda repositories. But I suspect people might face very similar problems with many R packages that are not provided in the R channel.

issue_title: conda update --all does not ignore anaconda version
issue_body:
In my previous understanding and practice, the `conda update anaconda` will update the anaconda to the newest version, and if I installed some new version of packages, they will be downgraded to the version that matches the anaconda version. On the other hand, `conda update --all` will ignore anaconda version, install the newest version of all other packages, and turn anaconda version into `custom`. That was how I updated packages previously. 
However, when I update anaconda to `5.0`, the `conda update --all` works the same as the `conda update anaconda`, it will turn my `custom` anaconda into `5.0`, and downgrade all my other packages. This happens both on my windows and osx laptop. I wonder whether it is a bug, or a new feature?

issue_title: fn + url in meta.yaml is redundant
OSError: [Errno 17] File exists
issue_body:
My Python development environment consists of a system version of Python (built using homebrew) and various environments created and maintained by conda. I know the warning I'm getting states that I shouldn't use conda in an non [ana|mini]conda environment, but I don't use [ana|mini]conda for my default python as I can't build things such as matplotlib from source due to issues in finding dynamic library dependencies. Up until recently, I had no problems as I never installed modules into the default environment via conda. I merely used it to switch between various environments.

After updating to conda version 3.7.4, I can no longer switch into different environments. I get the following error.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/conda", line 5, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/main.py", line 67, in main
    activate.main()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/cli/activate.py", line 99, in main
    conda.install.symlink_conda(join(binpath, '..'), conda.config.root_dir)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/conda/install.py", line 374, in symlink_conda
    os.symlink(root_conda, prefix_conda)
OSError: [Errno 17] File exists
```

If I go in and manually remove the **conda**, **activate**, and **deactivate** symlinks from the <env>/bin directory of an environment, I can switch in. However, the symlinks are never removed. I have to go into the environment's directory and remove the symlinks each time I want to switch to that environment.

Is this a bug or is this to prevent people from doing what I'm trying to do?

issue_title: Corruption of other environments when upgrading packages using pip
issue_body:
This seems more like an issue with pip/setuptools, but, anyway, it is affecting conda.

When mixing conda and pip, if a package is upgraded by pip, it will replace the _contents_ of the package files (instead of replacing the files with new ones). Because the files are hard-linked, the other envs that had the same package will be corrupted.  Here is a way to test this:

issue_title: miniconda/anaconda installers for tcsh and sh (bourne shell)
issue_body:
There are still people who use `tsch` or `sh` as login, and many times they are not even aware.

It would be nice if the default installers, for example Miniconda ones (https://conda.io/miniconda.html), would detect the shell environment and suggest the appropriate login profile. For example, in bourne shell it's `~/.profile` and tsch it's `~/.tcshrc`.

I could modify that part of the code myself and do a pull request, but I can't find the source of the installers anywhere in here. Are they available here or somewhere else? Google did not help me much either.

Thanks,
João

issue_title: conda install from file doesn't respect channels in .condarc
issue_body:
A file named 'conda-requirements.txt` has the following contents:
```
packageA
packageB
packageC
```

A .condarc file in the user's path exists as follows:
```
channels:
  - https://conda.binstar.org/some_organization
  - defaults
```
The binstar channel for `some_organization` contains the packages for `packageB`. Executing:
```
conda install --file conda-requirements.txt
```
Results in:
```
Error: No packages found in current osx-64 channels matching: packageB
```
A manual installation:
```
conda install -c some_organization packageB
```
is successful.

Seems like a bug.

issue_title: Conda command not initialised correctly when sourced from conda.sh script in Ubuntu 16.04
issue_body:
## Current Behavior Recently, conda started complaining about the following: $ /usr/local/anaconda3/bin/conda activate CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'. If your shell is Bash or a Bourne variant, enable conda for the current user with $ echo ". /usr/local/anaconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> ~/.bashrc or, for all users, enable conda with $ sudo ln -s /usr/local/anaconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh /etc/profile.d/conda.sh The options above will permanently enable the 'conda' command, but they do NOT put conda's base (root) environment on PATH. To do so, run $ conda activate I have done as requested and removed Anaconda's 'bin' folder from my PATH variable. However, the conda command is no longer available after login in Ubuntu 16.04. At first glance it may appear that '/etc/profile.d/conda.sh' is not being sourced correctly at login. However, adding the following line at the beginning of the script reveals that it does actually get sourced: ``` export THA_TEST=1 ``` since I can see the environment variable `THA_TEST` in my bash terminal after login anew. When I try sourcing the script manually from a bash terminal (in my home folder) after login, the script seems to work: ``` $ source /etc/profile.d/conda.sh $ conda [works correctly...] ``` This means something must be going wrong inside the script '/etc/profile.d/conda.sh' when sourced during login, but not when sourced manually in a bash terminal after login. ### Steps to Reproduce Simply do exactly as the conda warning requests in Ubuntu 16.04 and the 'conda' command should be unavailable afterwards. ## Expected Behavior I expect that the 'conda' command should be available in a my bash terminal after login in Ubuntu as a result of having been sourced from '/etc/profile.d/conda.sh' ## Environment Information

issue_title: az provider register should provide wait / no-wait
issue_body:
```bash
$ az provider register --namespace "Microsoft.Storage"
Registering is still on-going. You can monitor using 'az provider show -n Microsoft.Storage'
null
```

"You can monitor using `az provider show...`" doesn't seem like a reasonable way to handle a request for registration. If the rest of the script requires that resource provider to be registered, the consumer of this command will need to loop poling `az provider show` to determine the request result.

There should be wait / no-wait on this command. Wait should wait for the registration to complete and return either an error or the body of the provider.

issue_title: Can not use "space" or "=" in the "environment-variables" when creating container
issue_body:
### Description
I'm trying to deploy container in ACI, where I would like to use " " or "=" in environment variable.

When I'm using Docker locally this works fine:
```
docker run -it --rm -p 2222:22 -e SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS="ssh-rsa AAA...iM=" peru/ubuntu_sshd

$ docker inspect 2b3528737878
...
            "Env": [
                "SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS=ssh-rsa AAA...iM=",
                "PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin"
            ],
....
```

The command above is working fine. When I use the same using the "az container create" it can not handle the "space" nad "=" in the variable properly:

```
az container create --name mycontainer --image peru/ubuntu_sshd --environment-variables SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS="ssh-rsa AAA...iM=" --resource-group H...S --port 2222

or 

az container create --name mycontainer --image peru/ubuntu_sshd --environment-variables "SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS=ssh-rsa AAA...iM=" --resource-group H...S --port 2222
```
As you can see here the environment variable ```SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS``` is not properly parsed/handled, because it takes " " or "=" as separator:

```
az container show -n mycontainer
{
  "containers": [
    {
      "command": null,
      "environmentVariables": [
        {
          "name": "SSH_PUBLIC_KEYS",
          "value": "ssh-rsa"
        },
        {
          "name": "AAA...iM",
          "value": ""
        }
      ],
...
```

---

issue_title: ACR Build doesn't properly parse .dockerignore
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
I had a problem where `az acr build` would always send an empty context (75 bytes, and the `.tar.gz` file in `%TEMP%` was empty).
Turns out my `.dockerignore` was the problem, I've attached it to this issue so you can take a look at it, but it basically ignores everything `**` then un-ignores a few things.
[dockerignore.zip](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/files/2233024/dockerignore.zip)

**To Reproduce**
- Create a `.dockerignore` file that starts with `**` then add a few `!` lines to un-ignore the folders you want to add to the context.
- Run `az acr build`
- Notice that it sends a 75 byte context and it will fail to find your `Dockerfile`

**Expected behavior**
I expect it to parse the `.dockerignore` just like regular docker, and thus only send the folders I need while ignoring everything else.

**Environment summary**
azure-cli (2.0.42)
acr (2.1.0)
Docker Version: 18.06.0-ce
OS: Windows 10 Version 1803
Shell Type: Powershell and CMD

issue_title: tab completion: make it consistent and predictable
issue_body:
1. 'location' completion should be available across all commands, so fat it is only available for 'vm create'
2. It is slow
3. Print out non related lists if there are no list. 

============original feedback==============
One of the _wow_ pieces of feedback we always get is the ability to use bash completion for common parameter values, such as VM sizes, locations, etc.  The problem is that this works beautifully in some commands and not in others, so can never really trust it and rely on it for productivity.  The problem is exacerbated by the fact that statement completion is currently pretty slow and prints out a weird list in cases where there is no value completion.  For example if you type az storage account create –location<tab><tab> we print a list of all of the parameters the create commands takes followed by all files in the current directory.  But if you type az storage account create –type<tab><tab> you get a list of storage account types.  I think it’s prudent to flush out the model and use it consistently, such that users can intuit the behavior.

issue_title: Curl install script improvements
issue_body:
- [ ] Change default install location to a user location (e.g. `~/az`) instead of global location (`usr/local/az`)
- [ ] Prompt user if they want the script to modify their profile/rc file to update $PATH. If they decline, print instruction on how they can modify their $PATH.
- [ ] Fail install with appropriate message if custom install path contains space. Provide user friendly error message to address https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/1070

- [ ] Allow custom profile/rc file to be provided. By defaulting to a user location rather than global, we address https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/1009 as sudo will no longer be required. This work item extends this further so a user provided file can be used.
- [ ] Save tab completion script to install location and provide instructions on how user can enable tab completion later (addresses https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/939).
- [ ] After install, check $PATH for `az` and warn user if another `az` exists already on the system (e.g. from a pip install or apt-get install). We can warn the user and list the other azs found.
- [ ] If installation already exists in target directory, prompt user so we delete the old install directory.

Once the above changes have been made:
- [ ] Publish updated scripts to storage account so they're publicly available.

issue_title: No Longer a method to extract Activity Log for a resource
issue_body:
The `azure-monitor` python package contained the `MonitorClient`, capable of accessing `activity_logs` and filtering by resource_id, resource_group, etc. With `azure-monitor`'s deprecation, surely `azure-mgmt-monitor` would have this type of functionality? Nope. 

Even better, the hosted docs on microsoft's website state that the `MonitorManagementClient` of the `azure-mgmt-monitor` package _should_ be able to fetch `activity_logs`: [link](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/python/api/azure.mgmt.monitor.monitormanagementclient?view=azure-python). What gives microsoft? Why support so many language specific SDKs and keep them essentially out of sync with one another _and_ the PS cmdlets?

Just for fun, here is the output of `dir` on a MonitorManagementClient instantiated with the `azure.mgmt` modules on Python 2.7:

`[...<python_specific_outputs>..., 'activity_log_alerts', 'alert_rule_incidents', 'alert_rules', 'autoscale_settings','config','log_profiles','service_diagnostic_settings']`

Honestly, would love a Linus Torvalds-like audit of this repo, this is fourth inconsistency I've run into in a week's time between docs, code samples, the actual code, and the nuances of interfacing with sovereign clouds vs commercial.

issue_title: bad ESRI sources yield 0 row CSV files that don't look like errors
issue_body:
In a [run I did last night](http://com.somebits.oafull.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com/runs/1421723433.179/index.html) 27 of 578 sources resulted in a 23 byte out.csv file, something that just contains the headers. All of these sources have the same problem: "Downloaded 0 ESRI features" and a cache.json file that is an empty FeatureCollection.

One problem is that these ESRI sources aren't being processed correctly. It'd be nice to look at why. It could be the service is just broken in which case it'd be nice to signal that as a download error somehow.

The other problem is that the result collector should probably flag this kind of an output as an error. Maybe some fail safe in the reporting code that if the CSV file is too small it's assumed to be an error?

This is just a nice-to-have; it's pretty obvious to the user something went wrong. Here's the source list:

us-ia-polk us-pa-beaver us-ia-linn us-mn-polk us-mn-yellow_medicine us-mn-wadena us-va-roanoke us-tx-hurst us-in-madison us-mo-barry us-ga-gordon us-ct-avon us-nv-nye us-nv-lander us-nv-henderson us-tx-north_richland_hills us-mo-st_louis_county us-tx-colleyville us-tx-dallas us-va-fluvanna us-il-tazewell us-ar us-ct-lyme us-tx-keller us-mi-muskegon us-tn-memphis us-fl-alachua

issue_title: npm wrapper/bootstrap script
issue_body:
hi @asottile 

So I'm wondering if we could make it easier to get started with pre-commit, if you're just writing a JS library and don't already have a venv for anything else.

I've written a proof of concept and I'd love to get your thoughts - https://github.com/magicmark/pre-commit-wrapper-npm

(tl;dr output: https://i.fluffy.cc/SPL2sxrnVmHVXptGxV14B0NpS6qz4SPC.html)

This is an npm package which just wraps pre-commit, and makes a venv during the `install` hook.

Current Drawbacks:
- Because I'm not sourcing the auto-created venv, `pre-commit` could refer to some globally installed pre-commit instead, causing version conflict issues (I ran into this). Wondering if we can somehow make the `.git/hooks/pre-commit` always refer to the correct executable?
- If the repo hasn't `git init`'d, or there isn't already a `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, the wrapper package install fails when doing `install-hooks`. This can be improved with messaging though.
- Layer of indirection to the installation of `pre-commit` - harder to specify the version of `pre-commit`, unsure how to support respecting a custom pypi registry
- Relies on `python3` to be magically available (although gyp does the same thing with py27 - I think there might be a better way to tell npm/yarn to specify where python lives, I'll look into this)
- Any more that I've missed?

Pros:
- Reduction of boilerplate (no need to add venv creation to makefiles, reqs.txt, aactivator)
- Easier to get started with pre-commit in a js, non-python package

lmk what your take on this is. thanks!

issue_title: Hook contribution & minor suggestions
issue_body:
Hello,

I'd like to introduce you to 2 simple pre-commit hook I wrote:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks

It felt really simple to write, cleanly architectured and easy to share with colleagues.
So thanks a lot for your work on this !

Now, I have some minor complains:

- **no support for local hook development** : I had to create an initial github repo and then hack my changes in `$HOME/.pre-commit/repoXXXXXX/py_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pre_commit_hooks/forbid_crlf.py` while working on it. There should be an easier way to do this !

- the **required sha** property in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` : combined to the pain point #-1 above, it was really annoying to have to update it each time I was committing a new version of my hook on github. Couldn't you defined **a default empty value meaning to track the lastest version** ? I know I could use tags to specify stable versions, but it doesn't fit a work-in-progress development situation.

- the lack of instructions on how to develop a hook: your docs **never mention the necessity to have a `setup.py` file with entry points defined**, while you actually need it as a `virtualenv` is used under the hood. My [commit history](https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/commits/master) illustrates how puzzling developping a hook can become :D

What's your take on all of those issues, and which features would you be ok to integrate given that someone would suggest a pull request ?

And again, thank you very much for this awesome hooks manager !

issue_title: [client] Using connection pool without session or session without cookies
issue_body:
## Long story short

Sometimes one needs to make requests to a number of external http resources, some of them being client-facing API providing cookies you do not really need. The Session manages cookies by default and there's no simple way to easily switch it off. In fact, in many situations only connection pooling is required. Creating a session for every request does not look as a good approach.

A typical use case is a BFF, orchestrating multiple microservices.

## Expected behaviour

1) `aiohttp.ClientSession(disable_cookies=True)`
2) `aiohttp.ClientConnPool()` and then just `pool.get()` with same syntax as session.

If there is a simple way to achieve one of these, an example in the docs would be awesome.

Thanks in advace!

issue_title: URL Params are decoded too often
issue_body:
## Long story short

Passing `a=%25s3` as a URL parameter causes an exception, saying "ValueError: Unallowed PCT %s3"
`a=%25s3` is at some point decoded to `a=%s3` and tried to parse as URL, which is of course invalid.

## Expected behaviour

`a=%25s3` should not be decoded to `a=%s3`

## Actual behaviour

`a=%25s3` is at some point decoded to `a=%s3` and tried to parse as URL

## Steps to reproduce

I debugged the whole issue. It seems the problem is in the file _client_reqrep.py:77-81_, where (if i understand correctly) the passed params (as dict) are merged with the params already in the URL.
Here is a code snippet showing the issue.
```
In [19]: URL('http://foo.bar').with_query(URL('http://bar.foo').with_query('a=%25').query)
Out[19]: URL('http://foo.bar/?a=')

In [20]: URL('http://bar.foo').with_query('a=%25')
Out[20]: URL('http://bar.foo/?a=%25')

In [21]: URL('http://bar.foo').with_query('a=%25').query
Out[21]: <MultiDictProxy('a': '%')>
```
Also here is some testcode:
```
import aiohttp
import asyncio

async def main(loop):
    params = {'key1': '%25s3', 'key2': 'value2'}
    with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get('http://127.0.0.1', params=params) as resp:
            print(resp.status)
            print(await resp.text())

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main(loop))
    loop.close()
```

## Your environment
* Linux-Arch 4.8.11-1-ARCH
* Python 3.5.2
* aiohttp 1.1.4

     This includes project version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. -->

issue_title: Webfonts wasn't rendered in web page that use css and font file serving through runserver command
issue_body:
* **aiohttp-devtools version:**
0.6.4

* **aiohttp version:**
2.3.7

* **python version:**
Python 3.6.3

* **Platform:**
macOS

### Issue Summary
Text in web page was rendered using system fonts instead of webfonts that I have specified in css file.

I have checked that all files can be served correctly (got http status 200 or 304).

If i use webfonts library that was served from CDN, text was rendered with webfonts perfectly. This issue was happened only when files was served through `adev runserver` command.

issue_body:
I am doing a project about this
[http://www.androidauthority.com/build-google-assistant-raspberry-pi-770296/](url)

I used raspberry Pi3 to run the  command from the dev terminal: src/main.py
it shows: No such file or directory 
And i found "main.py" is  in /home/pi/vice-recognizer-raspi/src 
So i typed:  /home/pi/vice-recognizer-raspi/src /main.py
It shows:
Traceback "/home/pi/vice-recognizer-raspi/src /main.py" line 26, in <module> ImportError :No module named 'googlesamples' 

main.py:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# Copyright 2017 Google Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.

"""Main recognizer loop: wait for a trigger then perform and handle
recognition."""

import logging
import os
import sys
import threading
import time

import configargparse
from googlesamples.assistant   import auth_helpers

import audio
import action
import i18n
import speech
import tts

# =============================================================================
#
# Hey, Makers!
#
# Are you looking for actor.add_keyword? Do you want to add a new command?
# You need to edit src/action.py. Check out the instructions at:
# https://aiyprojects.withgoogle.com/voice/#makers-guide-3-3--create-a-new-voice-command-or-action
#
# =============================================================================

logging.basicConfig(
    level=logging.INFO,
    format="[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s:%(name)s:%(message)s"
)
logger = logging.getLogger('main')

CACHE_DIR = os.getenv('XDG_CACHE_HOME') or os.path.expanduser('~/.cache')
VR_CACHE_DIR = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, 'voice-recognizer')

CONFIG_DIR = os.getenv('XDG_CONFIG_HOME') or os.path.expanduser('~/.config')
CONFIG_FILES = [
    '/etc/voice-recognizer.ini',
    os.path.join(CONFIG_DIR, 'voice-recognizer.ini')
]

# Legacy fallback: old locations of secrets/credentials.
OLD_CLIENT_SECRETS = os.path.expanduser('~/client_secrets.json')
OLD_SERVICE_CREDENTIALS = os.path.expanduser('~/credentials.json')

ASSISTANT_CREDENTIALS = os.path.join(VR_CACHE_DIR, 'assistant_credentials.json')
ASSISTANT_OAUTH_SCOPE = 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/assistant-sdk-prototype'

PID_FILE = '/run/user/%d/voice-recognizer.pid' % os.getuid()


def try_to_get_credentials(client_secrets):
    """Try to get credentials, or print an error and quit on failure."""

    if os.path.exists(ASSISTANT_CREDENTIALS):
        return auth_helpers.load_credentials(
            ASSISTANT_CREDENTIALS, scopes=[ASSISTANT_OAUTH_SCOPE])

    if not os.path.exists(VR_CACHE_DIR):
        os.mkdir(VR_CACHE_DIR)

    if not os.path.exists(client_secrets) and os.path.exists(OLD_CLIENT_SECRETS):
        client_secrets = OLD_CLIENT_SECRETS

    if not os.path.exists(client_secrets):
        print('You need client secrets to use the Assistant API.')
        print('Follow these instructions:')
        print('    https://developers.google.com/api-client-library/python/auth/installed-app'
              '#creatingcred')
        print('and put the file at', client_secrets)
        sys.exit(1)

    if not os.getenv('DISPLAY') and not sys.stdout.isatty():
        print("""
To use the Assistant API, manually start the application from the dev terminal.
See the "Turn on the Assistant API" section of the Voice Recognizer
User's Guide for more info.""")
        sys.exit(1)

    credentials = auth_helpers.credentials_flow_interactive(
        client_secrets, scopes=[ASSISTANT_OAUTH_SCOPE])
    auth_helpers.save_credentials(ASSISTANT_CREDENTIALS, credentials)
    logging.info('OAuth credentials initialized: %s', ASSISTANT_CREDENTIALS)
    return credentials


def create_pid_file(file_name):
    with open(file_name, 'w') as pid_file:
        pid_file.write("%d" % os.getpid())


def main():
    parser = configargparse.ArgParser(
        default_config_files=CONFIG_FILES,
        description="Act on voice commands using Google's speech recognition")
    parser.add_argument('-I', '--input-device', default='default',
                        help='Name of the audio input device')
    parser.add_argument('-O', '--output-device', default='default',
                        help='Name of the audio output device')
    parser.add_argument('-T', '--trigger', default='gpio',
                        help='Trigger to use {\'clap\', \'gpio\'}')
    parser.add_argument('--cloud-speech', action='store_true',
                        help='Use the Cloud Speech API instead of the Assistant API')
    parser.add_argument('-L', '--language', default='en-US',
                        help='Language code to use for speech (default: en-US)')
    parser.add_argument('-l', '--led-fifo', default='/tmp/status-led',
                        help='Status led control fifo')
    parser.add_argument('-p', '--pid-file', default=PID_FILE,
                        help='File containing our process id for monitoring')
    parser.add_argument('--audio-logging', action='store_true',
                        help='Log all requests and responses to WAV files in /tmp')
    parser.add_argument('--assistant-secrets',
                        help='Path to client secrets for the Assistant API')
    parser.add_argument('--cloud-speech-secrets',
                        help='Path to service account credentials for the '
                        'Cloud Speech API')

    args = parser.parse_args()

    create_pid_file(args.pid_file)
    i18n.set_language_code(args.language, gettext_install=True)

    player = audio.Player(args.output_device)

    if args.cloud_speech:
        credentials_file = os.path.expanduser(args.cloud_speech_secrets)
        if not os.path.exists(credentials_file) and os.path.exists(OLD_SERVICE_CREDENTIALS):
            credentials_file = OLD_SERVICE_CREDENTIALS
        recognizer = speech.CloudSpeechRequest(credentials_file)
    else:
        credentials = try_to_get_credentials(
            os.path.expanduser(args.assistant_secrets))
        recognizer = speech.AssistantSpeechRequest(credentials)

    recorder = audio.Recorder(
        input_device=args.input_device, channels=1,
        bytes_per_sample=speech.AUDIO_SAMPLE_SIZE,
        sample_rate_hz=speech.AUDIO_SAMPLE_RATE_HZ)
    with recorder:
        do_recognition(args, recorder, recognizer, player)


def do_recognition(args, recorder, recognizer, player):
    """Configure and run the recognizer."""
    say = tts.create_say(player)

    actor = action.make_actor(say)

    if args.cloud_speech:
        action.add_commands_just_for_cloud_speech_api(actor, say)

    recognizer.add_phrases(actor)
    recognizer.set_audio_logging_enabled(args.audio_logging)

    if args.trigger == 'gpio':
        import triggers.gpio
        triggerer = triggers.gpio.GpioTrigger(channel=23)
        msg = 'Press the button on GPIO 23'
    elif args.trigger == 'clap':
        import triggers.clap
        triggerer = triggers.clap.ClapTrigger(recorder)
        msg = 'Clap your hands'
    else:
        logger.error("Unknown trigger '%s'", args.trigger)
        return

    mic_recognizer = SyncMicRecognizer(
        actor, recognizer, recorder, player, say, triggerer, led_fifo=args.led_fifo)

    with mic_recognizer:
        if sys.stdout.isatty():
            print(msg + ' then speak, or press Ctrl+C to quit...')

        # wait for KeyboardInterrupt
        while True:
            time.sleep(1)


class SyncMicRecognizer(object):

    """Detects triggers and runs recognition in a background thread.

    This is a context manager, so it will clean up the background thread if the
    main program is interrupted.
    """

    # pylint: disable=too-many-instance-attributes

    def __init__(self, actor, recognizer, recorder, player, say, triggerer, led_fifo):
        self.actor = actor
        self.player = player
        self.recognizer = recognizer
        self.recognizer.set_endpointer_cb(self.endpointer_cb)
        self.recorder = recorder
        self.say = say
        self.triggerer = triggerer
        self.triggerer.set_callback(self.recognize)

        self.running = False

        if led_fifo and os.path.exists(led_fifo):
            self.led_fifo = led_fifo
        else:
            if led_fifo:
                logger.warning(
                    'File %s specified for --led-fifo does not exist.',
                    led_fifo)
            self.led_fifo = None
        self.recognizer_event = threading.Event()

    def __enter__(self):
        self.running = True
        threading.Thread(target=self._recognize).start()
        self.triggerer.start()
        self._status('ready')

    def __exit__(self, *args):
        self.running = False
        self.recognizer_event.set()

        self.recognizer.end_audio()

    def _status(self, status):
        if self.led_fifo:
            with open(self.led_fifo, 'w') as led:
                led.write(status + '\n')
        logger.info('%s...', status)

    def recognize(self):
        if self.recognizer_event.is_set():
            # Duplicate trigger (eg multiple button presses)
            return

        self.recognizer.reset()
        self.recorder.add_processor(self.recognizer)
        self._status('listening')
        # Tell recognizer to run
        self.recognizer_event.set()

    def endpointer_cb(self):
        self.recorder.del_processor(self.recognizer)
        self._status('thinking')

    def _recognize(self):
        while self.running:
            self.recognizer_event.wait()
            if not self.running:
                break

            logger.info('recognizing...')
            try:
                self._handle_result(self.recognizer.do_request())
            except speech.Error:
                logger.exception('Unexpected error')
                self.say(_('Unexpected error. Try again or check the logs.'))

            self.recognizer_event.clear()
            self.triggerer.start()
            self._status('ready')

    def _handle_result(self, result):
        if result.transcript and self.actor.handle(result.transcript):
            logger.info('handled local command: %s', result.transcript)
        elif result.response_audio:
            self._play_assistant_response(result.response_audio)
        elif result.transcript:
            logger.warning('%r was not handled', result.transcript)
            self.say(_("I don’t know how to answer that."))
        else:
            logger.warning('no command recognized')
            self.say(_("Could you try that again?"))

    def _play_assistant_response(self, audio_bytes):
        bytes_per_sample = speech.AUDIO_SAMPLE_SIZE
        sample_rate_hz = speech.AUDIO_SAMPLE_RATE_HZ
        logger.info('Playing %.4f seconds of audio...',
                    len(audio_bytes) / (bytes_per_sample * sample_rate_hz))
        self.player.play_bytes(audio_bytes, sample_width=bytes_per_sample,
                               sample_rate=sample_rate_hz)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

issue_title: Canonical Content Registry on Blockstack
issue_body:
Hello, we are specifying a protocol for a canonical content registry. You can read a detailed proposal here: https://github.com/mine-code/canonical-content-registry. We are interested in building it on top of Blockstore to do the following:
1. Store unique CCID (canonical content identifier) in Blockstore
2. Append file instance metadata to CCID (see examples in [canonical-content-registry](https://github.com/mine-code/canonical-content-registry))
3. Tag a CCID with a nametag
4. Sign a [statement](https://github.com/openname/specifications/blob/master/profiles/profiles-v03.md#statements) stating a nametag belongs to an Openname profile
5. Append arbitrary metadata to CCID (hashtags, related CCIDs, etc)
6. Ability to easily interpret the above by a new node in the DHT

I wanted to open this issue to start a discussion about extending Blockstore to support writing metadata in the format above. What are the systems current limitations and what could be possible steps to extend blockstore?

Reading the README should provide some context. Let me know if I can clarify any of the ideas, and feel free to open CCR related issues on that repo.

@muneeb-ali @shea256 @jessewalden @moudy

issue_title: [AppVeyor] The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect.
issue_body:
Related: #129 and #139

As mentioned, the CI builds for this repo fail on this same error.  Ignoring the issuing of posix `find` (disabled by `-X gcov`), noting that I do **not** actually know where this error comes from, here is the _symptom_ of whatever it is that is broken in this tool for windows:

```
codecov.exe : The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect.
At line:1 char:1
+ & codecov -X gcov -f .\coverage.xml --name win64_cxx
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : NotSpecified: (The filename, d...x is incorrect.:String) [], RemoteException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : NativeCommandError
 ```

I have rigorously tested a variety of ways to call the command, and they all result in the same `The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect.` message.  This is problematic, because almost every way that you can run `codecov` will _successfully upload the results_ but give a **non-zero exit code**, which reports back as a failed CI build.

**Failing Ways to Call Codecov**

Brackets `[-X gcov [-f filename [--name my_name]]]` indicate that all permutations (with / without `-X gcov` and `--name my_name` and `-f filename`) have been tested (because I'm obsessive, and was kind of drunk).

- `codecov [-X gcov [-f filename [--name my_name]]]`
- `python -m codecov [-X gcov [-f filename [--name my_name]]]`

Basically, how you are supposed to run `codecov` depending on what you've done with `$PATH` etc.

**Super Hacky Workaround**

So I'm a powershell novice, but there is this thing called `Invoke-Expression` that lets you call a command.  Conveniently (really only for this use case actually), `Invoke-Expression` will give an exit code of `0` even if the thing that was invoked gives a non-zero exit code.

@johlju i don't really know if this enables an alternative solution for you since i'm not capturing the "result" here, but in my `appveyor.yml` i just cheated and did this:

```yaml
# relevant for below
environment:
  matrix:
    - PYTHON:    "C:\\Python27"
      TEST_NAME: "win32_py27"
    - PYTHON:    "C:\\Python27-x64"
      TEST_NAME: "win64_py27"
    - PYTHON:    "C:\\Python36-x64"
      TEST_NAME: "win64_py36"

# ... your install / test_script etc

# Why use Invoke-Expression like this?  Because I keep getting
#
#   codecov : The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect.
#
# Which reports the build as failed, but Invoke-Expression always succeeds xD
after_test:
  - ps: |
      $codecov_cmd = '& codecov -X gcov --name ' + $env:TEST_NAME
      Invoke-Expression $codecov_cmd
```

I will successfully receive updated code coverage reports to `codecov.io`, regardless of the fact that the expression fails.

Anyway, I tried to give as much detail as possible to hopefully help somebody narrow down what is actually going on.  It seems that if you want to fix windows support for this (epic and amazing) tool, you need to target whatever is triggering this `The filename, directory name, or volume label syntax is incorrect`, which I think comes from that `root = quote(root)` business (maybe don't do that unconditionally, but instead do it if `platform.system() != "Windows"`?

Hope this is helpful.  It's a quasi-duplicate, and I've finally found a workaround, so feel free to close it if you like.  I just wanted to elaborate on the precise problem, since I care very deeply about this tool and `codecov.io` :heart:

issue_title: IOStore directory compression slow
issue_body:
We know moving files around is going to be a bottleneck that needs tuning.  One thing that jumps out at me while testing on chr22 is how much time it takes to gzip directories when writing via the IOStore interface.  

For example, 
https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil-vg/blob/master/src/toil_vg/vg_index.py#L240-L241
takes about as much wall time as the indexing itself.  

Immediate fix is to turn off compression here (maybe by adding an option to write_global_directory).  vg and index files are already compressed anyway.

issue_title: Modify überscript to run HG19 and GRCh38
GATK wants karyotypic order for HG19
issue_body:
Well you know, your reference files might be in karyotypic order and it'd be a damn shame if your reads weren't in karyotypic order because that would be a completely unrecoverable error now wouldn't it be. I mean, since all of our inputs are indexed, there's no possible way that we could use the index to read  the BAM file in random order.

Also, since we validate the order of ref sequences listed in all inputs, there's no possible way that we could see our reference, known-sites VCFs, and reads have the same ref sequence order, and skip the karyotypic order.

Anyways, word on the street is that you can disable this with `--unsafe`. Going to add this to #187 as well.

Possibly the best part of this is that someone hardcoded both karyotypic order and code to recognize the HG19 reference build into the GATK.

issue_title: Set an environment variable with current test
issue_body:
When executing tests in CI, sometimes a test may hang indefinitely. One can look at the output using `-v` but that might not always be reliable.

If each worker would set a specific environment variable with the test it is executing, one can use a process monitoring library like `psutil` to figure out which worker is running which test. This could be a variable named something like `PYTEST_XDIST_CURRENT_TEST` which is set to the test node id. Alternatively, we could also add if the test is in the `setup`, `call` or `teardown` phase.

The implementation should be trivial and can be really helpful when diagnosing CI problems.

@RonnyPfannschmidt what do you think?

issue_title: How to create individual log files for each worker
issue_body:
I'm trying to figure out how individual log files can be created for each worker, in the same manor that pytest --log-file does when not using xdist.

Since the basic pytest --log-file opens with write, not append (see https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/issues/3813), there does not seem to be a way of getting a complete log file for a run using xdist with multiple workers.  However, even if it could be opened in append mode, the individual worker IDs would not be present, and having everything mixed together can be more difficult for troubleshooting.

I'm hoping that there is some way of creating a log file for each worker, but I'm not really sure how to do that, if it's even possible.  I tried setting up a logger in pytest_configure, but worker_id does not seem to be available at that time.

issue_title: @mock.patch decorator doesnt work properly with @pytest.mark.asyncio
issue_body:
Sample test code:

`tests.py`
```python
from unittest import mock

import pytest
import testmodule


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_async_mock_patch_contextmanager():
    with mock.patch('testmodule.foo') as foo_mock:
        assert testmodule.foo is foo_mock


@pytest.mark.asyncio
@mock.patch('testmodule.foo')
async def test_async_mock_patch_decorator(foo_mock):
    assert testmodule.foo is foo_mock  # FAILS


def test_mock_patch_contextmanager():

        assert testmodule.foo is foo_mock


@mock.patch('testmodule.foo')
def test_mock_patch_decorator(foo_mock):
```


`test_async_mock_patch_decorator` will fail, because `testmodule.foo` is `<function foo at ###>`, not `<MagicMock name='foo' id='###'>`

issue_title: Make a TCP server and a client fixture.
issue_body:
So you can do in a test:

    def test_foo(tcp_client):
        client = await tcp_client([port=ip_factory()], [ip="localhost"])
        await my_server.start(client.port, client.ip)
        await client.send(b'some_byte')
        assert my_server.has_done_stuff()


    def test_bar(tcp_server):
        server = await tcp_server([port=ip_factory()], [ip="localhost"])
        
        # repond b'somebytes' when the incomming data match the "on" test
        server.respond(b'somebytes', [on=lambda request: True])
        
        client = await my_client(server.ip, server.port) 
        assert client.send(b'some_byte') # get the expected answer 
        assert my_client.has_done_stuff()
        
    Very useful for end to end testing.

issue_title: Instructions for CentOS should use ~/bin
issue_body:
The instructions on https://www.microsoft.com/net/core#centos say:

```bash
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dotnet/cli/rel/1.0.0-preview1/scripts/obtain/dotnet-install.sh | bash /dev/stdin --version 1.0.0-preview1-002702 --install-dir ~/dotnet
sudo ln -s ~/dotnet/dotnet /usr/local/bin
```

In My Humble Opinion, I don't think it's wise to link a global `/usr/local/bin` to point to an executable in a user's home directory. This can cause problems on a multi-user system.

I think the instructions should be:

```bash
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dotnet/cli/rel/1.0.0-preview1/scripts/obtain/dotnet-install.sh | bash /dev/stdin --version 1.0.0-preview1-002702 --install-dir ~/dotnet
mkdir -p ~/bin
sudo ln -s ~/dotnet/dotnet ~/bin
```

issue_title: netcoreapp2.0 SCD console app targeting osx-x64 doesn't work on Mac OS X 10.10.4
issue_body:
# Issue Title

netcoreapp2.0 SCD console app targeting osx-x64 doesn't work on Mac OS X 10.10.4

# General

I created a simple "Hello World!"-app with .NET Core 2.0 like this (in a PowerShell command line window):

```powershell
> dotnet --version
2.0.0
> mkdir hwapp; cd hwapp
... snip
> dotnet new console
The template "Console Application" was created successfully.

Processing post-creation actions...
... snip
> code .
# In Visual Studio Code, select hwapp.csproj and add the following line below the TargetFramework:
# <RuntimeIdentifiers>osx-x64</RuntimeIdentifiers>
# Then continue with:
> dotnet publish -r osx-x64
Microsoft (R) Build Engine version 15.3.409.57025 for .NET Core
... snip
> Add-Type -Assembly "System.IO.Compression.FileSystem"
> [io.compression.zipfile]::CreateFromDirectory("X:\path\to\hwapp\bin\Debug\netcoreapp2.0\osx-x64\publish
", "X:\path\to\hwapp\app.zip")
# Transfer the file to the Mac, for example with (assuming curl.exe is in the PATH):
> curl.exe -kT .\app.zip https://transfer.sh/hwapp.zip
https://transfer.sh/123someId123/hwapp.zip
```

Download the file on the Mac (in my case a Mac Mini), extract the archive, right click on the file `hwapp` and select "Open with...", then choose "Terminal".

The following error message appears in the terminal window (some names and paths redacted):

```
Last login: <redacted>
<redacted>:~ <redacted>$ /path/to/hwapp/hwapp ; exit;
dyld: lazy symbol binding failed: Symbol not found: _clock_gettime
  Referenced from: /path/to/hwapp/libcoreclr.dylib (which was built for Mac OS X 10.12)
  Expected in: /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib

dyld: Symbol not found: _clock_gettime
  Referenced from: /path/to/hwapp/libcoreclr.dylib (which was built for Mac OS X 10.12)
  Expected in: /usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib

Trace/BPT trap: 5
logout

[Process exited]
```

So the message says that the SCD was built for Max OS X 10.12. But my understanding was that the advantage of the new portable RIDs introduced with .NET Core 2.0 was that the SCDs were actually portable across multiple versions of the target operating system. I understand that this doesn't work when using NuGet packages that don't support the target OS, but this "Hello World!"-app shouldn't be limited by that.

issue_title: storage.py should be more paranoid
issue_body:
`storage.py` often assumes that files exist in the right places (and that config files can be read and contain the required elements) because it creates and updates them correctly itself. But it's possible that a disk error could occur or that a user could tamper with the files after they've been created. In this case, storage.py should detect this and raise an appropriate exception, which other code that tries to work with lineages (like for renewal purposes) should be on the lookout for.

In theory we could check for file existence on *every* access, to avoid race conditions (for example, if one of the elements of a lineage gets deleted after the object is instantiated and before that element is accessed!), but at a minimum we should use the sanity checks whenever first instantiating a `RenewableCert` from a renewal config file.

issue_title: Key Agreement
issue_body:
We need to implement [Key Agreement](https://letsencrypt.github.io/acme-spec/#rfc.section.5.6) as per current ACME spec.

https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/issues/2203 (KDF) went through, but we're still blocked on https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/issues/1647 (ECDH). However, In general we are blocked on cryptography uploading the [Eleventh Release](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/milestones/Eleventh%20Release) - otherwise we will depend on their `master`, which is really suboptimal for our launch release; not to mention upload to distros, e.g. Debian.

cc @bmw, @hlieberman, @fmarier

issue_title: Handling new requests for supersets of names in existing certs
issue_body: Right now we choose a random (i.e., first-mentioned by `os.listdir`) lineage to suggest as the lineage the user might want to renew, in the case where there are several lineages within which the requested cert could be an enlarged successor cert. Instead, we should suggest the *largest* relevant lineage.

issue_title: Deprecate idiosyncratic tzstr format
issue_body:
Per [this question](https://stackoverflow.com/q/47874633/467366) on SO, unless we can find some sort of standard that matches up with `# GMT0BST,3,0,30,3600,10,0,26,7200[,3600]`, I think we can go ahead and deprecate it as a supported `tzstr` format (just in time now that @pablogsal has fixed it our support for it)!

I'm thinking we should

- [x] Add a `DeprecatedFormatWarning` on a successfully parsed string in this format (not a `DeprecationWarning`, I want people to actually see it)
- [x] Change all tests using this format so that they now check to make sure the warning is raised.
- [x] Remove this format from all tests where we're not *explicitly* testing our support for the format.
- [x] Remove any code examples or documentation related to this format, except possibly a `.. caution` note about the deprecation of the format.

I'm fairly confident that this won't cause major problems, since support for this format was inadvertently broken several releases back and we haven't heard anything.

issue_title: No longer works with FeinCMS
issue_body:
We are using FeinCMS with pages and content blocks which have a richtext field on their forms using the CKEditor. Because of a bug with this, I updated from `django-ckedtior-updated==4.2.8` to `django-ckeditor==4.4.6` hoping the bug would be solved, but instead, it has gotten worse. I noticed the textareas no longer have the `item-richtext` class anymore, and are now instead wrapped in a `div.django-ckeditor-widget`, but this doesn't seem matter much. The CKEditor works correctly on other models and on existing FeinCMS blocks of those pages, but not when adding a new unsaved block. In that case, CKEditor is not initialized at all and the field remains a basic textarea. I have properly set the `CKEDITOR_JQUERY_URL` setting.

I'm not entirely sure this is a bug in django-ckeditor, because I'm not entirely sure how FeinCMS and django-ckeditor are bound together, although I do see that the textareas are now wrapped, which is behaviour introduced by django-ckeditor.

I've tried installing from the repository (commit 615897a4), but then CKEditor doesn't work at all for me anymore, neither in the admin nor on regular pages. I'm getting the following error message:

```
ReferenceError: django is not defined
```

issue_title: Start should not be 0 after normalizing variants inserted before the start of transcript
issue_body:
When insertions occur before the first nucleotide of the transcript, the current normalizer would result in n.0_1insXXX, then the interval validator would fail as the start should never be 0. For example, 

when normalizing variant ''NM_212556.2:c.1delinsCA", the normalizer would firstly covert to n. representation of this variant "NM_212556.2:n.1delinsCA" (The CDS of this transcript starts just from 1 and the first nucleotide is A), then normalized to "NM_212556.2:n.0_1insC". When the normalizer tries to convert back to c. variants, the internal validator in n_to_c would raise exception as the start should not be 0.

issue_title: Create methods to register all component objects
issue_body:
Currently, definitions can be registered to be referenced to in the spec.

The spec allows to register other objects like parameters or responses.

OpenAPI 3: https://swagger.io/specification/#componentsObject

Some of them also exist in OpenAPI 2. Search for "Swagger Object" in https://swagger.io/specification/v2/.

We'd rather get this right with all those objects in mind rather than address them independently each time everyone needs one of them.

It should be easy to create methods to register each object type. Or should we create a single method and pass the type as argument (and deprecate definition)?

Then it's less obvious how to help the developer to use references. Referencing schema definition is automatic. Referencing responses probably wouldn't be, so the user would have to enter the reference response name. Ideally, we'd provide a function that at least takes care of the `$ref` syntax allowing the user to just enter the name of the ref.

False positive on Integer Overflow
Provide URL of related resource in schema when type="related"
issue_body:
Hello,

Please correct me if I have overlooked some options, but I believe that currently one cannot automatically build e.g. a Backbone-Relational client for a Tastypie API because related resources are not fully specified in the schema.

To be concrete, if I write code like this in the `my_api.resources` module.

```python
class FooResource(Resource):
    bar = ToOneField('my_api.resources.BarResource', attribute='bar')
    ...

class BarResource(Resource):
    ...
```

Then the resulting schema for `Foo` will include the following:

```
bar: {
   blank: false,
   default: "No default provided.",
   help_text: "A single related resource. Can be either a URI or set of nested resource data.",
   nullable: false,
   readonly: false,
   related_type: "to_one",
   type: "related",
   unique: false
}
```

It would be quite useful to also include a field like `resource_uri: /api/bar` (and also `schema_uri: /api/bar/schema` would be helpfully explicit rather than depending on Tastypie's URI structure). From browsing the code I believe this is as simple as showing something like `RelatedField.to_class().get_resource_uri()` but I would like to hear thoughts from core devs.

Thanks!

- Kenn

issue_title: PV ForecastModel - alternate data source question
issue_body:
Hi All!

Great to see a PV focused library with forecasting getting a lot of traction! Thanks and well done!

I'm currently working with a company that provides Irradiance and PV data (both forecasts and historical) derived (primarily) from satellite imagery and query-able from a simple HTTP API. We've had interest to use our data with this library.

After a look through your documentation and source code for `ForecastModel`, I wanted to get some feedback for an approach for integration.

The simplest might be just to build a separate library that follows the `ForecastModel` base class methods and provides the expected `DataFrame` return from `get_processed_data`. The other option would be to allow a more extensible data source for `ForecastModel` beyond just a thredds based server.

Would really appreciate some feedback if this is a good idea or something that doesn't suit the goals of this library. Solcast provides free access for researchers globally and want to make it easy to use with the tools researchers are already familiar with. More info on [our site](https://solcast.com.au/).

Thanks again.


issue_title: What is the status of the optimization stuff in the unstable branch?
issue_body:
Sorry for opening an issue, but I couldn't find any other way to contact the development team (there is a "contact us" link at http://research.microsoft.com/en-us/um/redmond/projects/z3/, but it just downloads some document about Windows Small Business Server 2003 Service Pack 1). 

What is the status on the optimization stuff in the unstable branch? Is there a timeline on when it will make it to a stable release? 

If it won't be there soon, is there a way to do optimization in the latest stable release with reasonable performance (I know that at worst I can set up a SAT problem and bisect it).

The kind of problem that I wish to solve is a pseudo-boolean problem. I have several SAT clauses and a linear expression with integer coefficients on some of the SAT variables which I wish to minimize. 

Specifically, I'm investigating Z3 for use in [conda](http://conda.pydata.org/) for dependency resolution. Conda already uses a SAT solver (picosat) to do dependency resolution, but the pseudo-boolean constraints are converted to SAT using BDDs (see "Translating Pseudo-Boolean Constraints into SAT", Eén and Söensson 2006). The SAT clauses represent things like "package A depends on package B", and the pseudo-boolean formula that is minimized lets the solver find a solution that has maximal versions installed. The formula is then minimized using bisection. 

The problem with conda's approach is that it is too slow for certain problems (it can take minutes to get a solution). We've looked at using an ILP solver, but there don't seem to be any good free ones for Python. I tried using Z3's optimization stuff (see https://github.com/asmeurer/conda/blob/clauses/Z3Opt.ipynb) to solve a problem that takes conda's current solver about 15 minutes to solve, and it solved it in a few seconds, which is plenty fast, and I haven't even made some optimizations yet (e.g., there are some carnality constrants that are represented in CNF, but could probably be used by Z3 more efficiently as actual cardinality constraints). (side note, why does it take 14 seconds to convert the clauses to Z3 Python objects?)

conda's current solver is a hassle to users when it hits a hard problem, and is preventing us from doing some things that we'd like to do with the solver. Z3 seems like it's a good fit, since it has Python bindings, works in Python 2 and 3 and all platforms, and is MIT open source.

issue_title: MergeLayer accepting dictionaries
issue_body:
Right now, our MergeLayer base class, the only layer class that can handle multiple inputs, requires its input layers to be given as a list. This is a good fit for the ConcatLayer, for example, which concatenates multiple input tensors in a specific order. In #522, we found that this is a bad fit for the recurrent layers, though, which accept multiple input layers meaning very different things: the actual input, a mask, and an initial hidden state. For this use case, it would be much more suitable to access the different input layers (and inputs, and input shapes) by name: that is, `incomings` in the constructor, `input_shapes` in `get_output_shape_for()` and `inputs` in `get_output_for()` should be dictionaries rather than lists.

What can we do to allow this?
a) Add another `MergeLayer` base class (NamedMergeLayer` or `DictMergeLayer` or something) that has a different interface and adapt `lasagne.layers.get_output()` accordingly?
b) Expand `MergeLayer` to accept either a list or a dictionary in its constructor and change its behavior accordingly? -> This would mean that existing merge layers need to cope with the possibility of being handed a dictionary.
c) ~~Change the `Layer` base class to always allow multiple input layers?~~ -> This wouldn't make sense for most layers such as the `DenseLayer`.

There's also the side issue of #337: We may want to allow a single layer to output a list or dictionary of tensors. How would that interplay with merge layers? Is is to be treated completely orthogonal (i.e., a merge layer is for merging multiple layers, not for processing multiple inputs) or do we need to account for this?


At first glance, a) seems like the cleanest solution, with the disadvantage that having two different merge layer base classes will be confusing, especially if we cannot rename the current one.

issue_title: dropout layer performance
How to get the gradient( dL/dw) of the first hiden layer during training?
The output size of TransposedConv2DLayer(Deconv2DLayer)
issue_body:
My input size is (14,14) and I want yhe output size to be (28,28) after implementating TransposedConv2DLayer. Here is an example:

network = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(shape=(None, 1, 14, 14), input_var=input_var)
network = lasagne.layers.Deconv2DLayer(network, num_filters=1, filter_size=(3, 3), crop='same',
            stride=2,  nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify)

But the actual output size is (27,27).
Then I read the source code which define the output size of TransposedConv2DLayer:
(output_length - 1) * stride - 2 * pad + filter_size    (1)

Here 'output_length' is actually 14. To get my expected output size, for simplicity, I just change (1) into:
(output_length - 1) * stride - 2 * pad + filter_size + 1   (2)

And it works. But I don't know whether this change is reasonable. Is there better way to do this?

* properly label development (or nightly) release

issue_title: MoneyManager can't survive Options._expire_cache()
issue_body:
**Steps to reproduce**:
1. Create a ``MoneyModel`` with ``MoneyField``;
2. Create a new model with ``MoneyField`` during the test or just in a runtime;
3. ``MoneyModel.objects`` is a Django's ``Manager`` instance.

**Expected behaviour**:
``MoneyModel.objects`` should be a ``MoneyManager`` instance.

The problem is, that ``Options.managers`` is a ``cached_property``, which is populated from ``Options.local_managers`` but we don't update this list and when the cache is cleared we have an unpatched manager again.

Cache is cleared when ``registry.Apps.register_model`` is called, which calls ``registry.Apps.clear_cache``, that leads to ``Options._expire_cache`` calls for every registered model.

``` python
class HomePage(Page, TranslatablePageMixin):

        verbose_name='claim',

    )

class SubmitFormField(AbstractFormField):

    body = RichTextField(blank=True, help_text='Edit the content you want to see before the form.')
    thank_you_text = RichTextField(blank=True, help_text='Set the message users will see after submitting the form.')

]
```

issue_title: angr usage question.
Don't use collate for encoding
Missing imports fail silently
issue_body:
I was getting `failed to parse uri` errors on CSV paths, but only in certain environments. I eventually figured out that this was because those environments were using a custom-built python that was compiled without `bz2` support, which caused the csv backend to fail on `import bz2`.

I know this is a fringe case, but it's one where a lack of warning or error messages cost me hours of work.

issue_title: vehicle.commands.count reports 1 lower than it should
issue_body:
when retrieving a list of mission commands, the count seems to always be one less than it should be.

So for example, if I load mission into Copter which is 2 waypoints, within ardupilot there will be 3 items in the command list: home, wp1, wp2.  It's arguably bad behaviour that command-0 is always home but this is besides the point.  From the dronekit user's point of view, when the command list is retrieved, there are actually 3 items that he/she can iterate over but the count is only 2.  this will lead people to not read the last command.

issue_title: overriding settings from the command line should only affect the current invocation
issue_body:
The `mpsyt set... , set...` feature is very helpful, but it seems to change mpsyt settings permanently, and this can lead to unexpected results in subsequent invocations of the program.

Scenario: as I do for many UNIX programs, I wrote a shell script wrapper for mpsyt, for a very specific need which requires overriding the standard settings via command line parameters. Currently, if I run mpsyt directly, after running my wrapper, mpsyt will keep using the special settings, instead of the expected standard settings.

The expected behaviour, like for standard UNIX programs, is that overriding settings by command-line options only affects the current instance of the program, without permanently changing settings in the program configuration file.

issue_title: PriceBoard缺乏统一的更新接口
issue_body:
rqalpha中的交易撮合是基于PriceBoard实现，在最新的行情数据Event触发前，PriceBoard就应优先更新，否则在撮合成交时基于的价格仍然是前一笔行情数据（行情订阅列表中，撮合成交要早于用户自己编写的strategy）。
目前代码https://github.com/ricequant/rqalpha/blob/master/rqalpha/core/executor.py在run函数的95行有bar数据更新PriceBoard的逻辑，但是tick数据并没有实现相应的接口。
基于rqalpha通过插件的形式实现tick数据回测时，在不改动executor.py源代码的情况下，无法实现正确的撮合成交逻辑。
建议提供通用的api接口，由插件编写者自己实现。

Is this class particularly used by people and does anyone know if this works?

issue_title: Font color barely readable on dark backgrounds
issue_body:
Some font colors are barely readable in combination with dark backgrounds (e. g. dark blue font color for the prompt).

As I understand the color schemes of ipython are used and there is a section in the default .gangarc where I should be able to configure ipython to use another colorscheme ("Linux", "LightBG", "NoColor", ...). uncommenting this and trying the different schemes has no effect on my session. I also deleted my ~/.ipython and ~/.ipython-ganga folders to cover the case that I misconfigured ipython somewhere.

Is there a way to change the color scheme or turn it off if it is not possible?

issue_title: MassStorage file problem
issue_body:
Problem found by LHCb user is:

```
I am using a fresh: v601r23 version and I ma getting the following error:

ERROR    Error Putting or cleaning up file: *.root, err::global name 'glob' is not defined

In the ganga submission script I did define the following:

j.outputfiles=[MassStorageFile(namePattern="*.root", outputfilenameformat=project+'/'+unique_production_name+'/{jid}/{sjid}/{fname}’)]

I think all looks OK and the files are at least stored on the desired eos location.

Can you please advise what to do?
```

The script used for this is:

```
import os

project = 'DiMuon'
configfile = "DiMuon4.py"

unique_production_name = '2011_21r1p1a_EW_up'

# for testing
#datalist = 'test.py'

# 2011
#datalist = 'LHCb_Collision11_90000000_Beam3500GeVVeloClosedMagUp_Real_Data_Reco14_Stripping21r1p1a_EW_DST_26_4_2016.py'
datalist = BKQuery('/LHCb/Collision11/Beam3500GeV-VeloClosed-MagUp/Real Data/Reco14/Stripping21r1p1a/90000000/EW.DST', dqflag=['OK']).getDataset()

projectpath = os.environ['HOME']+'/workspace/LHCb/'+project+'/'

j=Job(application=DaVinci( version = "v39r1p1" ), backend=Dirac() )

j.name = unique_production_name
print "\n["+j.name+"] Working on: " + j.name

print "\n["+j.name+"] Initalizing ganga running of project: " + project + " with unique_production_name version: " + unique_production_name
print "["+j.name+"] Using project path: "+ projectpath +"\n"

print "["+j.name+"] Config file: " + configfile
print "["+j.name+"] Data list in file/from query:\n"
print datalist
print "\n"

j.application.optsfile = [ File ( projectpath+configfile ) ]

j.application.extraopts="""
DaVinci().PrintFreq = 1000
DaVinci().EvtMax = -1
DaVinci().InputType = 'DST'
DaVinci().DataType = '2011'
DaVinci().Simulation = False
"""
print "\n["+j.name+"] Here are the changed job options: "
print j.application.extraopts

#j.inputdata = j.application.readInputData(projectpath+datalist )
j.inputdata = datalist

j.splitter = SplitByFiles( filesPerJob = 10 )
j.parallel_submit = True
j.outputfiles=[MassStorageFile(namePattern="*.root", outputfilenameformat=project+'/'+unique_production_name+'/{jid}/{sjid}/{fname}')]
#j.outputfiles=[DiracFile(DiracFile(namePattern='*.root',locations=["CERN-USER"])]

print "\n["+j.name+"] Writing the following files to EOS:"
print j.outputfiles

print "\n["+j.name+"] Now submitting the job ..."
j.submit()
#queues.add(j.submit)
print "\n["+j.name+"] ... submitting job done.\n"
```

This concerns me slightly as there is an error somewhere around the handling of complex file names which could do with tracking down.
I'm worried that files could be being copied multiple times or not at all or multiple times but once is broken.
Given this effects the integrity of the output data for the user I think we should be sure there are no problems around this in 6.2.0.
(This functionality should also exist across all IGangaFile object types eventually!)

issue_title: Tasks remove is not working 6.1.15
issue_body:
Hi all,

Certainly don't want to hold up the release from going out so this can be fixed in 6.1.16

Performing the following gets Ganga into a strange state. I suspect that the remove function is failing quietly rather than throwing an exception from somewhere.

```
tr=LHCbTask()
tr.remove(remove_jobs=True)
```
This doesn't remove the task from the tasks repo. (This is the same for ```Task()``` or ```LHCbTask()```)
Performing the remove again appears to hit a pause as the repository is waiting for itself to finish removing the object from the repository.

I'll do some debugging to work out if this could be a repo bug or not, but I haven't observed this in my normal working with the jobs repo.

issue_title: The documentation for running in production stores the kinto.sock in a tmpfs
issue_body:
Following the configuration for [running in production with uwsgi](http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/configuration/production.html#running-with-uwsgi), it advises to store the `kinto.sock` file in `/var/run/uwsgi/kinto.sock` which, at least on some distributions, is a tmpfs (temporary filesystem).

This makes uwsgi complain: `bind(): Permission denied [core/socket.c line 185]`

See also http://stackoverflow.com/questions/12778646/uwsgi-socket-permissions

issue_title: Converting between trajectories
issue_body:
Hi there,

I was wondering how one would convert between pytraj box types within pytraj.

For example, I loaded in an AMBER trajectory, whose trajectory description is:
```
pytraj.Trajectory, 1000 frames: 
Size: 0.011064 (GB)
<Topology: 495 atoms, 495 residues, 495 mols, PBC with box type = truncoct>
```

Whereas when I load in a PDB file, I get a description
```
pytraj.Trajectory, 1 frames: 
Size: 0.000011 (GB)
<Topology: 495 atoms, 495 residues, 495 mols, non-PBC>
```

What exactly does this difference correspond to?

Note: I'm trying to concatenate the trajectories so I can project the resulting combined trajectory using eigenvalues and eigenvectors obtained from the PCA function. But, I can't do this unless I can align the PDB structure to the AMBER trajectory, which I can't do in pytraj without them being combined.

Thanks

issue_title: [BUG] listSecrets() returns only the first page
issue_body:
If Table.scan would return more than 1MB, the result is paginated.
LastEvaluatedKey is set and scan() should be called again. 

[relevant credstash code link](https://github.com/fugue/credstash/blob/8ded48aa25f9ed80930bcbaba96bb5301bc31ae4/credstash.py#L211)
[boto docs](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/dynamodb.html#DynamoDB.Table.scan)
[aws docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/QueryAndScan.html#Pagination)

I would like to determine the actual gain and exposure times for the frames of the ZR300's fisheye camera. I am using librealsense `master` branch from this repository.

In order to investigate I modified the `cpp-config-ui` example a little bit to add output for every fisheye frame: exposure time in metadata of the frame, as well as exposure time and gain from `dev->get_options(...)`. issue_title: Running an LSTM Policy A2C Trained Model
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm trying to run a trained model for the LSTM and LNLSTM policies in A2C. I was able to get the basic convolutional neural net policy to work just fine, but when trying to step the model for LSTM, I get an error because of the state and mask parameters. The error seems to have something to do with putting placeholders in the feed_dict when running the TF session from the policy. Here is my full code and error: 
```
import time
import tensorflow as tf
from baselines import logger
from baselines.a2c.a2c import Model
from baselines.common.cmd_util import atari_arg_parser
from baselines.common.vec_env.vec_frame_stack import VecFrameStack
from baselines.common.atari_wrappers import make_atari, wrap_deepmind
from baselines.a2c.policies import CnnPolicy, LstmPolicy, LnLstmPolicy


def enjoy(seed, policy, fps=100):
    if policy == 'cnn':
        policy_fn = CnnPolicy
    elif policy == 'lstm':
        policy_fn = LstmPolicy
    elif policy == 'lnlstm':
        policy_fn = LnLstmPolicy

    env = wrap_deepmind(make_atari("MsPacmanNoFrameskip-v4"), clip_rewards=False, frame_stack=True)
    env.seed(seed)

    tf.reset_default_graph()
    ob_space = env.observation_space
    ac_space = env.action_space
    nsteps = 5  # default value, change if needed

    model = Model(policy=policy_fn, ob_space=ob_space, ac_space=ac_space, nenvs=1, nsteps=nsteps)
    model.load("lnlstm_policy_a2c_model.pkl")

    while True:
        obs, done = env.reset(), False
        episode_rew = 0
        while not done:
            env.render()
            time.sleep(1.0 / fps)
            action, _, _, _ = model.step_model.step([obs.__array__()], state = model.step_model.S, mask= model.step_model.M)
            obs, rew, done, _ = env.step(action)
            episode_rew += rew
        print('Episode reward:', episode_rew)

    env.close()


def main():
    parser = atari_arg_parser()
    parser.add_argument('--policy', help='Policy architecture', choices=['cnn', 'lstm', 'lnlstm'], default='lnlstm')
    args = parser.parse_args()
    logger.configure()
    enjoy(args.seed, args.policy)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

issue_title: Simple way to run trained policy with PPO1/PPO2/TRPO?
issue_body:
First of all, thank you for providing these great baselines!

I can train the policies for the various algorithms (PPO1/PPO2/TRPO) and see that average reward increases and loss decreases, but is there a straightforward way to then simply evaluate the learned policy on an environment with a bunch of different initial conditions?

In PPO2, there's a way to save the model checkpoint, but is there any documentation about how to load that checkpoint? If not, do you have any suggestions on how best I might add this?

This functionality seems to be included in deepq, where there is a train.py and enjoy.py for sample environments, and it seems simpler in that the training procedure generates a single .pkl file that can be loaded by the enjoy procedure in one line.

issue_title: access to context (request) in DjangoConnectionField.get_queryset()
issue_body:
We're subclassing ``graphene.contrib.django.fields.DjangoConnectionField.get_queryset()`` and ``graphene.contrib.django.fields.filter.DjangoFilterConnectionField.get_queryset()``. To do the permission checks we need ``request.user`` in ``get_queryset()``. Previously we got it from ``info.request_context.user``, but this is no longer available. And ``@with_context`` does not seem to work here.

Maybe I missed something. Is there a better way to do this?

Sidenote: In our case we're actually monkeypatching ``get_queryset()`` to gain globally enforced permission checks on all relational fields.

https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/da0927aa841635c5b44fb496497d80d26f916fbc/graphene/contrib/django/fields.py#L28

issue_title: errors during `Schema`'s `schema` property method extremely difficult to debug
issue_body:
Errors in `@property def schema` (and probably any other `@property` in that class) do not yield useful tracebacks; instead, python "helpfully" falls back to `__getattr__` and then fails in there, which is extremely unhelpful.  This appears to be a specific case of https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/issues/2108

Would it be possible to ditch the `__getattr_` use, or alternatively the `@property`s?

issue_title: async resolvers in graphene breaks using nested structures
issue_body:
Using python 3.6, linux+mac

I'm trying to fetch data via http but the nested call hangs - this can be replicated by using `asyncio.sleep`

Replication: *comment out `asyncio.sleep` in `fakeFetch` to see the difference*
```python
from flask import Flask
from flask_graphql import GraphQLView
from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
import graphene
import asyncio
import sys

app = Flask(__name__)

def Log(message):
    print(f"LOG: {message}", file=sys.stderr)

async def fakeFetch():
    Log('Wait a sec...')
    await asyncio.sleep(1) # Comment this out and things work
    return 'Async World'

class Bars(graphene.ObjectType):
    bar = graphene.String()
    async def resolve_bar(self, args, context, info):
        await fakeFetch()
        return 'Bar'

class Foo(graphene.ObjectType):
    bars = graphene.Field(Bars)
    async def resolve_bars(self, args, context, info):
        await fakeFetch()
        return Bars()

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    foo = graphene.Field(Foo)

    def resolve_foo(self, args, context, info):
        return Foo()

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)

app.add_url_rule('/graphql', view_func=GraphQLView.as_view('graphql', schema=schema, graphiql=True, executor=AsyncioExecutor() ))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()
```

Query:

```graphql
{
  foo {
    bars {
      bar
    }
  }
}
```
